text.title: OKR Cascade for Multi-Squad Product Core Rules
text.summary: Cascade a single company OKR set into 3-6 squad OKR sets with explicit cross-squad dependency edges, weekly check-in cadence, and a post-quarter retro that closes the loop.

rule.statement: Each squad has exactly 1 Objective and 3 (max 5) Key Results. Objective is qualitative; KRs are quantitative with baseline + target + measurement instrument.
rule.rationale: More than 5 KRs dilutes focus; <3 collapses to a single-metric goal.

rule.statement: Every cross-squad dependency MUST be a written edge in the cascade graph: {producer_squad, consumer_squad, deliverable, by_date}. Implicit 'we need their API' is forbidden.

rationale.source: John Doerr — Measure What Matters; Spotify squad model

rule.rationale: Implicit dependencies cause Q3 surprises; explicit edges surface them in Q-planning.

rule.statement: Every squad KR MUST trace to >=1 company-level KR. Traceless KRs are killed in planning.
rule.rationale: Squad KRs that don't sum produce green-squad-red-company quarters.

rule.statement: Weekly squad check-ins MUST update KR confidence (0-10) + name top blocker. Skipping the check-in for 2+ consecutive weeks triggers escalation.
rule.rationale: Quarterly-only OKR review reveals failure too late; weekly trends predict misses.

rule.statement: Post-quarter retro MUST score each KR (hit / partial / miss) and tag cause (right-target / wrong-target / blocked / under-resourced). Retro outputs feed next-quarter Objectives.
rule.rationale: Retros that don't tag cause produce no learning.

rule.statement: If applicability conditions fail (any Skip-If trigger holds — single-squad product; non-OKR framework; quarter <60 days; pre-PMF squad), the agent MUST skip this methodology and route to the named alternative or to the methodology fallback.
rule.rationale: Mis-applying a methodology outside its preconditions degrades output quality and pollutes downstream artefacts; the explicit skip-rule keeps the routing tree honest.
